findings.reachability: Any logged-in WordPress user if a third-party plugin hooks 'astra_notices_user_cap_check' and returns true; by design reachable by any admin otherwise.
findings.justification: The capability check is wrapped in apply_filters('astra_notices_user_cap_check', current_user_can($capability)), meaning any plugin or theme that does add_filter('astra_notices_user_cap_check', '__return_true') silently grants all logged-in users (or even unauthenticated users if nopriv were added) the ability to dismiss notices and write arbitrary transient keys with attacker-controlled names and TTLs via the set_transient() call on line 126.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: capability check wrapped in overridable filter
detection_pattern.description: Finds WordPress handlers where current_user_can() is the second argument to apply_filters(), making the capability enforcement overridable by any installed plugin. Any third-party code returning true from the filter bypasses the check entirely.

findings.reachability: Logged-in users who fail the capability check but pass silently; observable as a timing/response side-channel.
findings.justification: The capability check (line 114) silently returns with no response on failure, while the nonce check (line 118) responds with a JSON error. This inverted ordering means an attacker can determine whether they passed the capability check by observing whether they get a JSON error response or a silent HTTP 200 with no body, leaking information about user privilege state.

detection_pattern.section_name: Auth Logic: capability check before nonce creates info-leaking response divergence
detection_pattern.description: AJAX handlers that silently return on failed capability checks before the nonce check runs produce different observable responses for capability-pass-nonce-fail vs capability-fail, leaking privilege information to callers.

findings.reachability: Any client receiving the AJAX nonce-failure response.
findings.justification: esc_html_e() echoes its translated string to output and returns void (null). Passing it as the argument to wp_send_json_error() means the translated error string is written directly to the output buffer before wp_send_json_error() emits its JSON envelope, producing a malformed response like 'WordPress Nonce not validated.{"success":false,"data":null}' that breaks JSON.parse() in the client JS and prevents proper error handling. The correct function is esc_html__() which returns without echoing.

detection_pattern.section_name: Improper Output: esc_html_e() inside wp_send_json corrupts JSON response
detection_pattern.description: esc_html_e() echoes to output and returns null; using it as an argument to wp_send_json_*() corrupts the JSON body. Any plugin doing this will produce an invalid JSON response that breaks client-side error handling.

findings.reachability: Any authenticated admin who has visited any admin page (where the nonce is injected into every admin page load via enqueue_scripts).
findings.justification: A single shared nonce action 'astra-notices' (created at line 149 and verified at line 118) covers all notice dismissals regardless of notice_id. Because the nonce is injected into every admin page load, a valid nonce for dismissing one notice is equally valid for dismissing any other notice. If a more-privileged notice existed (e.g., one with capability 'edit_plugins'), an admin user could use a nonce obtained from any page to dismiss notices for capabilities above their own if the capability fallback logic produced a weaker default.

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF: shared nonce action not scoped per resource
detection_pattern.description: Finds nonces created with a static string action rather than a per-resource string (e.g., action . item_id). Shared nonces allow a nonce obtained from one context to authorize a different but same-type action.
